[IMAGE:0]It's just before midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"Nearly twenty-five centuries have passed since the burning of the Pythagorean Institute at Krotona.  One by one the old Mystery Schools have disappeared.  The truth seeker of today may no longer beg admission at the temple gates. The disciple must discipline himself... Each man is now his own high priest, his own initiator, the master of his own metaphysical life.  Having realized this, your own integrity must strengthen you for the path of discipleship." 
 
That's from Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment by Disciplines of Realization, arguably Hall's best book and one of the classics of the Golden Age of American occultism. (The image to the left, on the other hand, is one of Augustus Knapp's splendid illustrations for Hall's The Secret Teachings of All Ages.)     

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- see you next week!***

replies.title: Re: The Magical Battle of Halloween
replies.content:
JMG, what are gets?

—Lady Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: The Magical Battle of Halloween
replies.content: Every post on a message board on the chans is automatically assigned a number by the software -- say, 23453679. In the early days of 4chan, users turned repeating digits -- say, the 555 in 73465558 -- as a kind of game; when you got one, that was a "get." Two digits in a row is a "dub," three a "trip," and so on. Once the Kek Wars started, users started to notice that any post about Trump got an unusually large number of gets, and gets started being used as a mode of divination -- if you had a get, especially if it's more than a dub, then the assumption was that the frog god Kek was signaling his approval.  The time that somebody posted "Trump will win" and got the post number 77777777 kind of put the icing on that cake! So what our anonymous commenter is saying here is that there's reason to think that Kek has taken a far from sympathetic interest in the working the Magic Resistance is planning this Halloween, and fireworks can be expected...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, prophecy and divination are two different things. Diviners use symbols to grasp the subtle patterns shaping events. Prophets get their messages straight from the gods. The diviner goes looking for omens; the prophet is told what to say.

replies.title: (no subject)

Seconding our host here. The Old Testament has some pretty explicit descriptions emphasizing that the prophet is told what to say.

For example, the first few chapters contain a wild description of YHWH giving Ezekiel a scroll written full of woe and horror, and telling him to eat it. He does. And it tastes sweet to him.

The god gives you the words and they become part of you, and then you speak them. Even if you don't want to (Jeremiah actually argues with YHWH about this--says he doesn't want to prophesy anymore, but he's compelled to. I think Jonah has a similar experience.)

comments.title: Plants and Progress
comments.content:
Salutations JMG!

1) Since "The Sign of the Outhouse Moon" is dropping your books, will there be any plans to print a new edition of The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic alongside the new edition of Monsters with updates based on what you've learned since you wrote the first ENM? I'm about to go through crossing out and correcting the differing entries in ENM with the updated data from The Celtic Golden Dawn, and wondering what a new edition of ENM would look like.

I wanted to report a stunning success with my CGD practice working on breathing the light in and out of my body. Although it may ruffle some feathers, I ended up applying a bit of Buddhist rhetoric to release tension and emotional trauma from my body. My first day restarting practice I just worked on breathing the light in and out. Something strange has been happening ever since I started the course - when I start focusing my mind to meditate, scenes from an old movie called "Batteries Not Included" keep popping into my mind. It wasn't a particularly good or interesting movie, and I'd rather forget it. I figured trying to force it out of my mind too forcefully the first couple times created some kind of accidental memory link between those old memories and meditation, so I've been doing my best to ignore it and refocus. This time I tried to meditate on what this memory might mean, where it might have come from, etc. I came to the conclusion that "this isn't me" in the Buddhist sense, that this memory was not a core part of "me", that the emotions and patterns of thinking it always spawned whenever I remembered it were hollow echos and reflections of my real thinking. Other intrusive memories of movies surfaced, and I gave them the same treatment, zeroing in on a sort of "centre" of my consciousness where I could sense my will and direction came from. As I was doing this, my right leg (the one with major blockages and tension) began to buzz and tingle with the feeling of energy, almost as if by releasing those thought loops I was releasing more of the blockages that have plagued me for many years.
Today while working on releasing tension and inhaling/exhaling the light before discursive meditation, I realized that I was absolutely full of tension. I realized that all this tension came from the way I dealt with life - tense and precise, as if constantly trying to execute a perfect German goosestep - and realized that this tension had come from my early experiences in life. I realized that, although these things had happened to me, they weren't part of me, and I didn't need their negative aspects to accomplish my goals. One breath at a time, I released so much tension it felt like I was a fluffy cloud compared to the tight and tiny bundle of tense muscles and twitching nerves I was before. Closing the temple took on a joyful sacredness, and for about an hour afterwards I felt more peaceful contentment than I've felt in my whole life (I also got a bit of discursive meditation done on the first sphere of the tree of life). I can't wait to go again tomorrow morning!

2) Through the rest of the day though, I felt similar patterns of mind and tenseness of body slipping back in, and I've been re-relaxing and bringing my mind away from familiar loops all day. Aside from regular practice, is there anything that can help "lock in" the improvements that come from magical development to prevent old patterns from slipping back in?

replies.title: Re: Dwarves
replies.content:
Thanks for the book recommendations, I'll look into those.

I've actually been visiting a Heathen FB group, with several seidr workers for the last month or so. The group has a slice of academics as well, so I've been reading a variety of Nordic and Germanic papers recommended off of Academia.org.

One in particular caught my notice:
"Between a Rock and a Soft Place: The Materiality of Old Norse Dwarves and Paranormal Ecologies in Fornaldarsögur" by Daniel Sävborg and Karen Bek-Pedersen, 2018

The authors make some interesting observations on dwarves/dvergr in the history text, both Nordic and into Christian English period. They theorized that dwarves are actually a type of stone spirit, associated with a specific location/stone home. Also while dwarves and fae were accused of causing illness, dwarves also had a reputation for healing as well. Considering that many medieval medical texts used stones and crystal associations for healing, it made sense.

The authors also had thoughts on dwarven craft work and smithing that fit with my own thoughts on metal working. I used to do handcrafts and knife work, and am going to restart doing that. I'll have a workshop set up next Spring, for now I'm starting a couple of hand made bronze bladed herb knives. I had a good rep with the pagan community back then as someone who could turn out a proper ritual tool so I'm looking forward to getting my fingers dirty again.

My re-beginning of that kind of work is one of the reasons I'm interested in investigating a relationship with the Dvergr. I'm in no rush though, since I have no desire to disappear into a hillside and return a hundred years from now.

comments.title: A dream of Bastet
comments.content:
Good evening JMG, I have posted on other magic Mondays several times already, but being a young beginner I am still figuring out my way in magick and the metaphysical. I have two questions for you if you don’t mind:

1. Before I have asked and tried to incorporate Christian theology into my practice, forcing myself to use the trinity in my sphere of protection, but it never really stuck or felt right. (for probably very obvious reasons) Recently, just a few days ago, I had learned about Bastet, the Egyptian feline goddess. I was intrigued in her immediately and felt drawn to her. The day after I had a dream about myself going through tunnels of ice that were about to cave in and at the last minute, one of her followers had saved me and brought me to her. I cannot recall her exact words, but she had said something about “the worship of her is the expression of self” could this be a sign?

2. I am drawing away from technology and deeply resenting it’s presence in my life, I think this is a great thing, but I am worried about being able to relate to the people I care about when they are still “plugged in” so to speak. Is there a way to be comfortable with my views and interact with the technological lovers that are in my life?
Thank you so much for your answers I really do appreciate them!

replies.title: Re: A dream of Bastet
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's a typical sign -- and those words are very typical of her, for what it's worth. You might consider praying and burning some incense to her.

2) It's going to be a challenge. A lot of tech addicts are very uncomfortable with those who aren't addicted, and will get very nasty about it at times. You'll simply have to maneuver your way through the resulting difficulties one step at a time.

replies.title: Re: A dream of Bastet
replies.content: Thank you! Is there any particular incense or prayer that I can use to pray to her?

replies.title: Re: A dream of Bastet
replies.content: Frankincense and kyphi are both traditional. As for a prayer, the crucial thing there is to listen at least as much as you talk. I don't happen to know any traditional prayer to her, so simply speak to her as you would to a wise and powerful person, and then be silent and see whether you get a response. It may come in the form of feeling!

replies.title: Re: A dream of Bastet
replies.content:
I've been working with Bastet since the first of the year. I've volunteered with a local pet rescue and foster cats for the last three years and when I became more active in my occult practices, started praying to Her as a patron of the animals I work with.

While I actually work primarily with another deity, I set a small place on my altar for Bastet, with a small statue and a stone cat. She gets a brief prayer in the morning and evening with the others I do. For incense I normally burn Mugwort as a general protective and cleansing herb, nothing specific to Bastet.

As for what to say in your prayers, just be honest and talk like you would to a respected teacher or older family member. She hasn't seemed to require any floral praise or complicated hymns sung. Must be doing something right, our adoption rates are up slightly, along with out intakes. Its been a chaotic year for all rescues due to the pandemic.

You might consider volunteering yourself with a rescue, and help cats as a way to show Bastet your respect. Etsy has some good Bastet statues. Find one you like and speaks to you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not the OP, but I've been wondering about the lottery thing: you strongly advise against using magic to win the lottery but have said it's OK to use magic to find buried treasure.

I assume the issue is that the magic is against the rules of the lottery -- not explicitly, necessarily, but perhaps covered by some catch-all clause, or at any rate it would be considered cheating. While the winnings aren't strictly owed to anyone in particular, a fair outcome is owed to everyone who bought the ticket. By the Raspberry Jam principle, then, you open yourself up to being cheated in not-explicitly-against-the-rules ways.

On the other hand, treasure that's been long abandoned doesn't belong to anyone, so the Raspberry Jam effect is simply that someone else may claim some other opportunity before you can - an outcome that any reasonable person would accept.

Does that make sense?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes. Also, the lottery winnings will inevitably be handed out at a given time; the buried treasure could conceivably stay buried until long after our species is extinct. Thus no one necessarily loses by your winning.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a fine example. The thing to keep in mind is that a lottery is by definition a situation where one person wins and everyone else loses -- it's a strictly zero-sum game. If you avoid doing magic to affect zero-sum games, and more generally try to stay away from such games and look instead for positive-sum games (games in which everyone benefits), you'll do better.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: Trading?
replies.content:
I'm curious as to how you would view trading whether it's arbitrage or financial instruments, with the lens of the Raspberry Jam principle.

In the case of arbitrage, if I find a shoe cheaper at a retail store compared to Amazon, I list it and sell it, I guess I open myself up to people undercutting me too.

In financial trading, let's say I buy a call option on the stock, I'm explicitly saying that I think the seller (or whoever is net short) is on the wrong side of the trade.

In both cases I think if one understands the risks, it's completely fine, and correspondingly fine for one to use magic for either purpose too?

replies.title: Re: Trading?
replies.content: I don't recommend using magic in a trading context. In theory, it ought to be no more damaging to your karma than any other unfair advantage, but in practice people who do it get immediate gains followed by massive losses. Do you recall the people who tried to use affirmations et al. to make a mint in real estate in the runup to the 2008 crash? I knew quite a few of them, and they all ended up losing their shirts -- most had to declare bankruptcy and lost most or all of their assets. That sort of thing has happened often enough that I recommend staying out of financial magic!

Not many people understand the being of money and its energy.

In other words, it is a form of alchemy, which is its own discipline. It goes beyond spells etc.

replies.title: Re: Trading?
replies.content:
I must ask now, what about the saying "millionaires do not use astrology, billionaires do"?

How is it different to use magical means to move money your way, than to move yourself to where money already is? Or did all those billionaires provide nothing but commonly needed goods and services for a fair price?

replies.title: Re: Trading?
replies.content: Astrology is a source of insight into what the market will do, no different in principle from any other form of technical analysis. It's also available to everyone -- again, like other forms of technical analysis, the data is out there in public and the principles can be found in books anyone can buy. What Morgan meant by that slogan, btw, is that if you use astrology when playing the market your chances of becoming a billionaire go up. It's less colorful but equally true that if you own a hot dog stand and use astrology as a source of guidance for your business strategy, your chances of becoming a successful hot dog vendor go up, too.

replies.title: Re: Trading?
replies.content: Financial astrology uses information that's available to everyone (the position of the planets).  It's no more unfair than getting ahead because you're the only trader who bothered to read the newspaper and work out what the information implied.

replies.title: Re: Trading?
replies.content:
Done properly, finance isn't always a zero-sum game. For example, carrying risk has a cost, and that's the logic of homeowner's insurance -- it's easier for the insurance company to carry the risk than it is for an individual. Likewise, accurate information has value. If you trade to make a company's valuation more correct, you're producing a benefit to everyone. (Or to take a more controversial example, short sellers have an incentive to root out and expose fraudulent companies.)

So if you proceed thoughtfully and ethically, there isn't necessarily a problem.

My non-occult $0.02 on 3.a.


1. "Math" isn't a monolithic thing, so find your specific strengths. Personally, geometry isn't my strong suit, but I can work wonders with a matrix.


2. Puzzles and exploratory work are good ways to build up skills.

In April on the other blog (https://www.ecosophia.net/on-the-far-side-of-silence/) there was talk of a homework problem: “Here is a square. Divide it with four lines, two horizontal and two vertical. How many squares do you have now?” Assuming it's not limited to the boring case of equally spaced lines, how many different numbers of squares can you construct? Are there patterns to which numbers can be made? Can you find any techniques to make it easier to construct a particular number?

If that particular one doesn't interest you, do a different one. FiveThirtyEight has a weekly puzzle feature on Friday morning that might be another source to start with.


3. You don't have to follow the order it's taught in school. Side trips are encouraged! If you can get through it -- it's not complex, just sometimes counterintuitive -- learning the basic ideas of infinite set theory is a good way to have your mind blown, for example.

Happy hunting!

replies.title: exercise for journaling
replies.content:
Here's the assignment that got me over this same block.

Write three pages in your journal every single day. If you can't think of anything to write, literally write "I can't think of anything to write about" over and over and I guarantee you that eventually something else will come out. Write nonsense. Write whatever comes into your head but fill those three pages. Set yourself an achievable period of time for the experiment, I recommend twelve weeks. Don't go back to read any of it until the end of that time. At the end of the experiment, reassess. Hope this helps.

-Korellyn

comments.title: It's official.
comments.content:
Hello JMG and all. I suppose this doesn’t qualify as an esoteric-related posting, but I’m not sure where else to post it. Maybe I’m enough of a regular to deserve some slack. Anyway, it’s official:

My medical practice will not renew its lease after 12-31-2020. Not only can I not afford it, the owner is selling the property to the hospital across the street, which will redevelop it. So I’m out of business. As far as I know, I am the last Physical Medicine & Rehab doc in my state in outpatient clinical practice (notwithstanding interventional pain specialists); as of Jan 1, 2021, there will be none. Yeah, I saw this coming from a long ways off, but I’m startled at how stricken I feel.

My revenue stream is down 50-60% and falling. My staff and patients are too scared of Covid-19 to schedule any of the electrodiagnostic procedures that I perform, or schedule any disability rating exams; that’s one of the hits to my revenue. Because of Phase 2 lockdown rules, and the fact that my practice is “non-essential”, the lawyers say we shouldn’t see patients in person under any circumstances. There is no path to recovering a normal, in-person workload for the foreseeable future, so my patients have to be seen online, which generally means I can’t bill my usual rate, and even then, there are frequent technical glitches causing the encounter to not actually transpire. If that weren’t enough, my board certification expired, and while I’ve passed all the tests, and jumped through most of the hoops required for renewal, my specialty board also requires a “Practice Improvement” research project in which the renewal applicant has to come up with a research proposal to “improve clinical practice”, implement it, gather the data, write a paper on the results, and if it passes muster, then you get your board certification renewed. I didn’t do that project; I’ve been up to my eyeballs in alligators for years, the last thing I (or my overworked staff!) have time for is to jump over some arbitrary bar. And it is one high bar. So I had figured worst case, I could just fall back to retaking the exam that graduating residents take for their initial certification. That actually used to be an option, but I learned too late that it’s been discontinued. My board certification is irrecoverable. I’m really done.

As I mentioned recently on the other blog, I had long been angling to become either an electrical power engineer (for which I am credentialed), or an electrician apprentice. At age 62, there are no openings for me, so I’ve decided to start my own business as a sewing machine repairman. I’m now taking a correspondence course to learn this trade, and plan to hang up my shingle by March, 2021. It might even turn out to be a best fit for my Aspergers brain; wouldn’t that be a blessing!

Because of all this, to speak nothing of issues with my daughters, I’m pretty busy, and have not been able to as deeply engage with this blog, especially regarding Will, and also regarding Hesse on your other blog. Both of these topics are as near and dear to my heart as anything you, JMG, have written since May, 2006.

Needless to say, my situation has proven quite a workout for my Will. As difficult as things are, I am diligently applying myself to the Will exercises JMG has been outlining.

Please wish me luck and blessings. I’m more than open to intercessory prayers if you are inclined to offer. Please allow that I am a devotee of the Helenistic Pantheon, my patron deity is Hermes. Over the years, many of you have written me some kind responses, and if I didn’t thank you then, please know that I am grateful.

— Lunar Apprentice

replies.title: Re: It's official.
replies.content:
Hi (Anonymous)

Truth be told, I really don’t think choosing to be a sewing machine repairman is limiting. In fact, there seems to be quite a bit of opportunity. The only independent sewing machine repairman in my town is 81 years old, and he charges $80/hour, 2 hr minimum. There are several technicians who work out of the local sewing machine retail store as employees, but they focus on warranty work, and servicing recent models. If a customer comes in with an old machine, the techs say (per instructions of their employer) “oh, this is so old, parts are hard to come by and are expensive when you can find them at all. Why don’t you look at our brand new machines over here…”, which are always plastic and computerized. Many people prefer their old metal, non-computerized sewing machines, and are compelled to ship them out to the nearest big city 100 miles away, and cool their heals for 2-4 months before getting them back. And it’s expensive. But they do this.

There are 90 million sewing machines in the US, which ballparks to one machine per 4 people. My metro area has 250,000 population, which suggests around 60,000 local machines. Let’s say 10% of the owners are serious sewers (pronounced “so-ers”), that’s 6,000 local machines in need of annual servicing. Let’s say I capture 10% of them, or 600 machines per year. Thats only 60% of full time capability. At 2 hours per machine, $60/hr, that’s $72,000 per year; certainly less than I’ve generally made as a doctor, but more than I’ve been making since Covid-19. I can’t think of any other ready, open market requiring clearly defined mechanical skills and aptitudes, certainly not a market with such easy entry. (If you know of any, let me know!)

As far as the correspondence course, I need to learn this material somehow, and the course seems quite good and comprehensive. There is real-time technical support available after you graduate! The proprietor of the course is well into his 70’s; I’m not sure how much longer he’ll be around! I’m delighted to find such a quick, easy and cheap path into the trade.

Yes, I’m scared of embarking on this new venture. But I have to do something! This does seem promising, and when all is said and done, I really am looking forward to it.

I plan to develop machine-shop skills on the side, as I anticipate such skills will become pertinent at some point in the slow, ongoing collapse. I have a well-equipped lathe with accessories for gear-cutting, a metal band saw (for cutting blanks from metal stock), bench grinder, drill press and arbor press. I’ll be starting several projects to build miniature steam engines (real working ones) to develop machine shop skills, including skills to perform milling machine operations on the lathe, as I will forego an actual milling machine.

I plan down the line to set up small foundry to at least cast metal feed-stock from scrap metal; if that pans out, I'll look at producing real castings.

Thank you kindly for your input.

— Lunar Apprentice

That's a far kinder comment than you realize. Your comment made me aware just how much I focus on my failures. In fact, I've scored some dramatic rescues and saves in my career, which are satisfying in retrospect, especially the cases where I caught something another doctor missed... like my neurosurgical patient who was having agonizing headaches after her low back surgery, which no one took seriously. Hmmm... I thought, let me read her op report..Hmmm, the surgeon mentions he tore the dura, but he sewed it up... Hmmm... maybe it's leaking cerebrospinal fluid, CSF, causing her headaches, I better order an MRI of her low back ... and bingo! The MRI showed gobs of leaked CSF between tissue planes in her low back. So that tear WAS leaking. The neurosurgeon scooped her off my service, went back in and fixed it.

Other cases include several saved lives, and averting impending paraplegia in a 22 year-old woman who had been repeatedly misdiagnosed.

After I recalled those cases, I thought to myself, "yeah, you got a couple of those, but there weren't that many really". Then I was able to recall offhand a whole slew of cases like that, and at that moment, I noticed how my mind is predisposed to spin a negative narrative about myself. I'm often told by others how self-critical I am, but this is the first instance I've actually observed it directly. I really need to examine that mental process. It's like catching a regularly returning burglar for the first time.

I wish I knew the first thing about you Anonymous, but, truly, thank you for the reminder.

comments.title: Representations and Lifeworlds
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

In my attempt to understand the nature of representations, culture and shared cosmologies, i've contrasted your first batch of Well of Galabes essays with the Scottish anthropologist Tim Ingold. He writes around the same scientific bias which comes out in anthropological studies, where practicing cultures are seen to have 'invented' or projected their imagination onto the landscape. The researcher always writes from the vantage point as having unique access to 'real reality,' and sees cultures as 'pinning on' meaning. Often this works itself out as a cultural relativist position. Ingold goes against this current and talks about, among other things, how a cosmology comes out from moving through and against a landscape, emerges as a process of 'revelation' (like Gregory Bateson's ecology of mind) and are situated in relational contexts which are skilled and interactive (like hunting), rather than imagined through a process of metaphysical contemplation and then subsequently enacted. My main question was in your passage here: "Neither the presence nor the absence of life mind and meaning in the cosmos is given to us in the raw materials of experience; both are essentially arbitrary, culturally determined ways of assembling the raw material of sensation into the representation or mental construct that each of us calls 'the world.'" I'm interested because is this the late-stage realisation in the 'Dragon age' of culture, and Ingold's anthropological examples deal with cosmologies still within the Unicorn/Phoenix stage of culture? Is the necessary progression a realisation that the cosmology, which emerged from the human integration and interaction with the landscape, is essentially an arbitrary relationship staged by mind for the process of creating a livable lifeworld?

My question was more of a request; i'd like to know how far you like Ingold's position and what you would add or supplement it with. I think too of don Juan who talks about the process of 'stopping the world' when it comes to pulling the handbrake on our seamless 'flow of perceptual interpretations' when we are inside a culture and cannot 'see' outside of its representations. The bias of the scientist is that he believes he stands outside both 'nature' and 'culture' and has a unique vantage point to see 'reality-as-such' but that's, as far as I understand it, not the case. And following Roszak, the 'mindscape' which we in the West act, sense and think within is a cultural fabric stitched together by Science, littered with all the inert symbols of the past, inaccessible because our vision has fundamentally changed. I'm trying to pull this together in a clear piece of writing and would appreciate anything you have to say again on the topic.

(Here is a PDF where I am reading Ingold's work (chapters 1 and 3 deal specifically with this issue): https://leiaarqueologia.files.wordpress.com/2017/08/the-perception-of-the-environment-tim-ingold.pd

replies.title: Re: Representations and Lifeworlds
replies.content:
In order to address Ingold's position in any detail I'd have to put in the time needed to study his work and get a clear sense of what he's saying and what implications he draws from his take on things. What you've presented seems reasonable enough. So-called "cultural relativism" is simply dogmatic scientific materialism in pseudohumanist drag -- it tacitly assumes that the scientific materialist view of the world is objectively true, and so (for example) gods and spirits are cultural constructs rather than nonhuman realities.

As for the three colorfully named ages from my Galabes posts, no, not exactly. We're at the end of Western civilization's Phoenix age -- the second half of a Phoenix age in a literate society is always an Age of Reason -- and the first stirrings of a Dragon age are appearing around us. Many of the traditional societies studied by anthropologists are deep into their Dragon age, having established a stable relationship with their environments centuries ago. The difference is that we're still caught up in the rationalistic fantasies of the waning Phoenix age, in which it was (and always is) standard practice for people to disregard the evidence of their own senses in order to impose an imagined order on the world of phenomena. In a Dragon age that goes by the wayside; consistency takes a back seat to comprehensiveness as the Dragon amasses his hoard and slowly drifts off to sleep on it.

comments.title: Position of the hands during meditation
comments.content:
Dear John,

My wife recently took up meditation. She was introduced to mindfulness during coaching sessions at her workplace and, after reading Eckhard Tolle's 'The Power of Now', she has eventually settled into a routine. She meditates early in the morning, just after waking up and having her first coffee cup (!), and has been doing it for a couple of months now. She claims to find it very beneficial and helpful in managing her anxiety - or at least being more aware of it.

The other day, however, she told me that when she meditates her hands feel as if they grow bigger and incredibly heavy. It didn't bothered her, but she said it was a very noticeable sensation. She meditates just sitting on an armchair, with her back on the seat, feet on the ground and her hands loosely on her lap. I suggested that she tried keeping her palms upwards and see if it made a difference.

After a few days, she reported that her hands no longer feel heavy or big, but that she feels like a 'handful of warmth' on her palms, as if holding a light, but palpable sphere of warmth in the cup of her hand. She has always had very warm hands and she often jokes that she should pick up Reiki healing or something similar.

Interestingly, however, for over a decade now she has also had keratoderma on her hands and feet and all medical therapies have had exactly zero effect on her.

I'm wondering if she has some energy blockages in her limbs / extremities - and, if so, is there anything she could try to unblock them?

replies.content: Hey, not JMG, but I have experienced this.  Buddhists call these sensations Piti.  The hand thing has only happened to me right before, during, and after meditative absorption states called jhana. The warmth is more common but still a good sign as far as meditation goes.  If your wife can keep focusing on her object of meditation and relax while all this is going on, she might have some really pleasant states ahead of her.  It might be beneficial to not say too much to her about it as well, because desire for these states makes them much harder to achieve.

In my experience, this is part of the process and not something to be avoided, but ultimately to be harnessed and directed, though it would take a more experienced initiate to explain how.

She should seriously consider reiki or some similar approach, as (among other things) that will finish the process of unblocking her hands; heal others, heal yourself...

replies.content: Thank you, John, and the other commenters. I'll tell her to keep doing what she's doing. As for Reiki, I'm not sure how serious she is when she jokes about it, but her hands can be truly hot at times.

comments.title: Monasticism
comments.content: What would you consider to be the main pillars of a monastic lifestyle? In a Christian context monks seem so worried about avoiding temptations for normal things like relationship to other people and family. I feel like they are missing the point.

replies.title: Re: Monasticism
replies.content:
I think you may have got it backwards. It is not that monks did focus too much on avoiding the temptations in the lifestyles of the common people. Rather, the mystic's inner work they were pursuing requires a heavy investment of both time and effort; so much that the normal commitments of a standard lifestyle would be incompatible with it.

It is also a factor that monasteries are, for the most part, self sufficient communities. That requires a great amount of skilled labor in order to live a simple yet non precarious life. I suspect many junior monks were accepted not because they were well suited for a mystic path, but for the hard work they would be able to perform on behalf of the monastery. To these individuals, the discipline rules of the order would feel much more like arbitrary impositions. Though if they applied themselves to it anyways, they may be better suited to be a mystic in a future reincarnation.

replies.title: Re: Monasticism
replies.content:
In Europe the early years of monasticism took dedication and hard work. Later when rich people felt like acquiring merit by founding a monastery the life got a little easier-bread because it was the rule, not because it was the only food available. But well off families often used abbeys and convents as a dumping ground for surplus offspring--daughters they didn't have the money to dower, the younger son who wasn't likely to make it as a freelance knight. Sometimes these members were actually given to the monastery as children (oblates). They couldn't actually take vows until adulthood, but after being raised in the expectation that this was to be their life I doubt that many tried to leave. Well off people could also treat a convent or monastery as a retirement home, donating a sufficient sum for their support in return for room, board and care in old age. Large abbeys often owned large tracts of land some of which was tilled by serfs--so the abbot served the same function as a secular lord. Ans some abbots participated in politics and even rode into battle. As with any institution there were probably deeply dedicated and spiritual monks and nuns, some for whom it was a better 'job' than being a serf, others dedicated to arts such as music and calligraphy, others who had been forced into the life by family or other circumstances and hated moment of it.

For more recent example of abbots serving as major landlords and exercising secular power over peasants consider Tibet before Chinese occupation.

Rita

comments.title: Astrology and Seduction
comments.content:
JMG,

1. Is there any validity to the kind of astrological information you see in newspapers? Stuff like “Leos tend to have [xyz] personality and get along well with [Scorpios]” or “You have a challenging week coming up if you are Sagittarius” and the like? I know astrology works, and no doubt it is far more complex than the oversimplification you see in a newspaper column, but do they have some value in learning about yourself or what might happen?


2. I once read (on an obscure internet forum), the posts of a man who claimed he was basically able to seduce any woman he wanted with a 100% success rate. He said he did it so much that he eventually burnt out on too much sex and luckily settled down with one woman and had children and was happy now. Normally, I would be very skeptical of claims like this for the same reason that I am skeptical of get-rich-quick schemes and perpetual motion machines.

However, I am no slouch at seduction myself, and I have slept with over 100 women and dated many many more. I am very familiar with the process, and I know all the scams and systems that are marketed to desperate men. Leaving aside the fact that this guy wasn’t selling anything (he posted anonymously for a few weeks and deleted his account), he came across as very credible to me in a thousand different ways, based on my experience even though I don’t know how it is possible.

To give an analogy: I have read your comments before about Vine DeLoria’s book setting out accounts of magical feats that you don’t know how to perform and which sound impossible, but as an experienced mage, you still find the accounts credible. I’m in a similar position.

The reason I am posting about this here though, is because it is very interesting to analyse it from a magical perspective and I wanted to ask JMG if it sounded plausible from that perspective.

Anyway, so what it comes down to is that he would basically tell stories that had *nothing* to do with romance or sex, but had very powerful emotional undertones and then simply wait for the woman to throw herself at him (physically).

He basically said - in simplified form - that if you can make a woman feel strong emotions, then she will want to sleep with you, because it is so rare to find a man who can do that.

But I can see there’s a lot of skill involved. He had the highest mastery of being able cold-read women I have ever heard of. Specifically, he was so attuned to their emotional states and expressions that he was like an Olympic athlete in terms of deducing the emotions that particular woman would respond to. This is the heart of the system and it’s a skill to be developed - it’s not easy. As with incompetently done magic, the most likely result of doing it badly is “nothing”. So for example, one woman might have very strong emotional reactions to stories about fear and near-death experiences. Another to warm emotions like taking care of puppies..whatever (but NEVER anything related to love/sex/romance).

There is nothing to suggest he had any magical training or knowledge, but my reading of this from a magical perspective is that he was basically creating powerful astral images that generated strong emotional reactions and attuning it to the particular woman. One of his maxims was that you never had a logical conversation (mental plane) as it would detract from the goal of emotional stimulation (astral plane). I think JMG posted recently somewhere about practicing to cultivate emotional states in yourself and feeling the emotions that result from specific stimuli like music etc. That’s essentially what this guy does - cultivate emotions in others in a very targeted way.

There is also something relevant in one of Dion Fortune’s books (I can’t remember if I read it in The Magical Battle of Britain or Psychic Self Defence) - she talks about how you transmit ideas directly to another person’s subconscious mind and “seed” them there so that the seed grows and the person does it as if it is his own idea. She emphasises that it is irrelevant what the conscious mind thinks (what this guy would call “don’t talk in logical terms”) - it only matters whether the right points are transmitted to the subconscious (using images, not words, with no negatives) and whether it then takes seed there. But you have to be careful because if the “seed” (suggestion) is too alien or too different to what the recipient’s personality actually wants, then it will fail and not grow into the recipient’s own idea from the subconscious.

I am somewhat thinking aloud as I write this, but does all of this make sense from a magical perspective?

PS - If anyone reading is curious, yes I have briefly tried this guy’s system. For reasons I won’t bore everyone with, I didn’t try it exactly as he suggested, and I suspect that affected the results - as JMG often says, if you follow a system of magical training you should follow it as given and not change things you don’t understand. But even with my modifications, I had a fair few puzzled/neutral/WTF responses, a large number of women who responded and were engaged and interested but showed no immediate sign of wanting to sleep with me, and one spectacular success which basically went exactly as the guy said it would work after you learnt the system (the woman basically jumped me and started kissing me about an hour after we met and demanded that I take her back to my apartment and sleep with her immediately, and she bought the drinks for both of us too). I have no doubt that if I put some serious time and effort into practicing his system, I could achieve dramatic results (although I don’t know if I will ever get the results he claims to have had).

replies.title: Re: Energy lines
replies.content: Leys, not lays!  My take here differs from that of the popular authors on the subject. As far as I can tell, leys are not naturally occurring patterns -- the natural course of energy in the landscape follows curves, not straight lines. Leys were manufactured by human action in ancient times. Thus they exist in some places and not in others. Northwestern Europe and Japan have a lot of them, but some other areas have none at all. Where they exist, they can be useful, but they can also be dangerous -- it's a bad idea, for example, to build a house across one of them, as ill-health will follow. Their original purpose and destiny remain unknown.

replies.title: Re: Shared House cusp
replies.content: Um, there's no such thing. A cusp that's one second of arc past 29° Aries 59' 59" is at 0° Taurus 0' 0". It's either one or the other. Some astrologers say that if you've got a cusp in the last degree of a sign -- say, 29° Aries -- you should check to see if Venus rather than Mars rules that house, but that's because birth data is notoriously inaccurate (everyone present at a birth has much more to be concerned with than the exact moment the baby comes out) and so, if the birth was a bit later, the real cusp might be 0° Taurus.

comments.content:
Earlier this Monday someone asked about consecrating a ring using the formula for consecrating the wands. I wanted to piggyback on that and ask about extra consecrations generally.

It occurred to me as I was consecrating my wand, sickle and egg that:

1. We have here three consecration rituals. One creates an active tool of magic, which can project any of the five elements; one creates a passive tool of magic, which absorbs magical influence and enhances magical workings; one creates a protective tool of magic, which drives away evil or unbalancing magical influences. And so,

2. Each of these three formulae could have any number of possible uses. The first thing I thought was of building a Druid temple, either of wood or stone, with appropriate material placed throughout, and then consecrating the whole thing via a working similar to the consecration of the Druid's egg. On a smaller scale, an altar, rather than a whole building, could be so consecrated-- or a special extra consecration done for an altar. Another possibility would be to consecrate gargoyles or standing stones using the sickle formula, in order to create a protective guardian for buildings, or for pieces of land. Another, more complex, possibility would be to create statues consecrated as recipients of a passive influence, and then to use either the formula of conjuration to invoke a specific spirit into them, or else an invocation of an element or sphere in order to invoke the influence of a deity into them. And beyond this, a whole range of possibilities opens up.

Do you think that this kind of thing would work? Am I on the right track here?

replies.title: Re: General Consecration
replies.content: Excellent. Yes, you can work with consecration rituals that way.

comments.title: Just luck, or external assistance?
comments.content:
I recently got an email from a woman I'd never met, asking whether I might help her distribute the contents of her late father's ham radio collection. Being a proud member of the ham radio fraternity/sorority, I agreed to help find worthy homes for whatever was useful, and respectful disposal of that which was not.

In the collection, I received a large number of vacuum tubes, some operable vacuum-tube equipment, and some relatively modern public-safety communications scanners. My first thought was that my good behavior was being whimsically rewarded by The Powers That Be! (This, despite my prior public skepticism of vacuum tubes...)

My second thought was that public-safety communications might be an important thing to be aware of in an era of potential civil unrest, and that tubes are generally regarded as more robust to EMP conditions, so maybe the Powers That Be are more interested in preparing (rather than rewarding) me for good works.

What say you?

Lathechuck

replies.title: Re: Just luck, or external assistance?
replies.content: Good question. Divination might settle it. In the meantime, you're very right about the value of tube-based equipment when there's a risk of EMP!

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

On previous Magic Mondays, when someone has asked you whether it’s a good idea to perform a variation of a common magical practice, one of your most frequent answers has been to encourage them to experiment and find out for themselves. Why are certain practices, particularly the rituals in the Golden Dawn tradition, so amenable to individual variation while maintaining their effectiveness? In other pursuits, doing things accurately and precisely is important – for example, in chemistry, reactions have to be carried out in a specific way or else they fail – yet this doesn’t seem to be the case in magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Magic works with the mind, not with chemicals, and you can't standardize minds the way you can standardize ingredients in a chemical experiment. The point of having people try variations is so they can adapt the technique to the vagaries of their own mental structures.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Thanks as always for doing this. Would the following make for a good affirmation? “I release my obstacles and accept abundance and success.”

Thanks for your time,
Ryan M.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, that kind of affirmation is one of the reasons that so many people in the New Age scene have failed so dismally. To say that you release your obstacles doesn't mean that they go away, it just means that you're no longer keeping them in place -- they may stick around for reasons of their own. In the same way, you can accept abundance and success all you want, but that doesn't induce them to show up.

This is vague and passive; an effective affirmation is specific and active. To construct one, start by getting to know your obstacles. What are they? What holds them in place? What will happen if one or another goes away? (Be realistic about this; some obstacles are protective and some are reflections of genuine limits.) Then choose one you're ready to do without, and use an affirmation such as "I overcome (obstacle)!" Or if it's wealth you're after -- weasel words like "abundance" mean far too little, far too vaguely -- use one like "I see opportunities to make money wherever I look!" That's the sort of thing that will get you good results.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Much appreciated! Thank you!

Ryan M.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I can think of no way to find out. There's certainly something there, a force that appears to be shaping events in a very specific direction, but whether it's the Egyptian god Kek, the Great Old One Tsathoggua, or what have you -- how could any human know?

2) You might want to start by thinking about what you mean by the phrase "a real goddess." That's a much more subtle matter than you may realize. You can certainly invoke Lady Liberty and get a potent response -- I know people who do this -- and yes, divination is a classic way to answer such questions.

3) I think the word you're looking for is "saint." In ancient Greece the word was heros, but too many people interpret that as "hero" in the modern sense, not as what it actually was -- a person whose life showed the signs of divine power, and who can answer prayers after death.
